data.title: Ormat Technologies: The Pure-Play Geothermal Company Is Having Its Moment
data.description:
In 1904 Prince Piero Ginori Conti, working at a site in the town of Larderello, near Pisa, Italy, was able to light five light bulbs using his experimental generator that harnessed steam coming out of the earth. A decade after that, the first commercial geothermal power plant facility was commissioned nearby, generating 25x as much electricity (250kW), enough to power about 50 modern day homes.

Today, more than 120 years later, Larderello remains a working geothermal site, with 34 power plants operated by Itay’s global utility Enel (OTCPK:ENLAY), with a total capacity of 800MW supplying roughly 2% of Italy’s electricity – or enough to fulfill a small country’s needs (like El Salvador, which also has significant geothermal supply).

Geothermal power generation, at first glance, looks like a perfect solution for cleaning up electricity grids. Using nothing but the heat from the ground below, the technology adds zero emissions and can generate baseload (24/7) regardless of whether it rains, shines or blows outside. It is the only "truly contained" thermal base load power. All other, including nuclear, require fuel arriving from outside the power plant (whether by pipe, truck, train or ship).

Even though geothermal power has been deployed successfully around the world, it remains a niche segment of most grids. Only a few countries rely on geothermal for more than 10% of their electricity needs. But growth in installed capacity has marched along decade after decade. That is because, even though it is not cheap to build, it has the lowest "all-in" lifetime cost out of comparable, low-emission (but dispatchable) alternatives:

As is widely understood, re-configuring the electricity matrix is one of the most important steps in controlling greenhouse gas emissions. This can be done with existing technology, unlike other areas like commercial air travel, shipping and cement production – where no commercial solutions are readily available to significantly reduce emissions.

For electricity grids, the solution will necessarily include all types of zero or low emission technologies, including more nuclear, more CCGT (with carbon capture), more solar, wind and hydro (especially pumped storage upgrades, where possible). And more geothermal.

More of everything renewable will be needed to replace coal, unabated gas and (in emerging markets) fuel oil. And that is before the small issue of growing demand. Whether driven by data centers – in developed world - or growing middle income populations in developing countries, demand for electricity is sure to grow, and likely faster than during the last few decades.

So, we have the building blocks for an investment thesis at hand. We have a niche market (geothermal power) with a practically certain and very long runway for growth (fostered by transition policies). And importantly, a dominant player, with vertical integration and a long track record: Nevada-based Ormat Technologies, Inc. (NYSE:ORA)

ORMAT's Business

The US is a leader in Geothermal generation, with roughly 1/4th of the global installed capacity. According to EIA, The US has 40GW of potential and only 10% has been developed. Most of the resource is in Western states, where Ormat has its HQ and most of their power plants.

If you are doing anything geothermal, anywhere in the world, you will not go far before running into Ormat Technologies. Starting as an EPC contractor 60 years ago, the (originally Israeli company), has become vertically integrated, owning equipment production facilities and power plants. In the US, the leader in geothermal, Ormat is the leading producer (by a hair, but still...).

As of 2024, most of the Ormat's revenue and margin is coming from electricity generation (80% of revenues), with 16% coming from equipment sales and the remaining 4% from battery energy storage systems (OTCQB:BESS).

The +1,200MW power generation assets are mostly in the US (some 200MW are solar PV and recovered heat). On top of these, the construction pipeline includes 116MW of new geothermal projects coming online in the short term (2025-26). Over the medium term, the global installed capacity will grow 400MW to 500MW by 2028.

The BESS business is ramping up to scale as well. The company has a clear project pipeline that will add some 700MW of new storage capacity, all in the US – these are key grid stability assets that don’t add generation but compensate for intermittent renewables like solar and wind. There is a big opportunity in this market for Ormat, helping spread around solar power beyond daylight hours and buy valuable minutes for backing-up wind in Texas.

According to the company’s medium-term plan, which goes out to 2028, EBITDA generation will improve from about +$500 million currently to $800 million, with an incremental capital investment of about $2.2B (implying a reasonable 14% EBITDA margin on incremental capex).

Valuation and Leverage

The current valuation looks steep and is turning off many investors. It earns a solid "F" from the Seeking Alpha crowd. Although the multiples are indeed high, peeling back a layer leads to a more measured interpretation.

The company has a enterprise value of about $7B. comprised of $4.5B market capitalization and $2.5B in net debt. Both have been reasonably stable through the bast three years:

The first conclusion is that debt is not too high nor growing. With EBITDA of around $550 million, and net debt or $2.5B, a 4.2x multiple is not too taxing given the long-term contracted nature of most generation assets. The weighted average (and 90% fixed) rate on debt is below 5%, implying narrow spreads and good quality power offtakers.

The following is a table of current installed capacity and its corresponding replacement cost:

The most important assumption is the $4,500/MW for geothermal. In support, we have the recent example in Ormat's $88 million acquisition of the 20MW plant called Blue Mountain (Nevada), in operation since 2009, at $4,400/MW price.

Assuming the “products segment” which has $120 million in revenues and $300 million backlog is worth around $500 million, the whole company (based in installed capacity replacement value) would be worth $6B.

That makes the $7B valuation look less excessive, but still "toppy". To get comfortable with it, one needs to consider the development pipeline and its likely impact on valuation (not considered in the analysis above).

Planned project execution will take EBITDA into $800 million for 2028, only three years away. The short time means most of the projects are under construction or in advanced permitting already.

At valuation levels similar to today's (and perhaps helped by lower future interest rates), The additional $300 million in EBITDA will create $4B - $3.6B in additional enterprise value at 14x (today’s multiple) or 12x (normalized). Both are higher than the capex of $2.2B. Put another way, the EBITDA multiple would need to compress to 9x on $800 million EBITDA for enterprise value to remain flat.

At a normalized (lower) 12x EBITDA multiple, the 2028 enterprise value would be $9.5B. If net debt remains around $2.5B, the market cap would increase to $7B, a 55% increase from todays $4.5B.

Management explicitly pointed out (during 2024 investors day) that all capex could potentially be funded with internally generated funds, with 2028 debt ratios targeted at 3.0x. I don't expect they will fund growth that way, some debt makes sense and excess cash might be used to purchase shares or increase the dividend (in both cases contributing to returns obviously).

In any case, nice returns are still possible or even likely, depending on how you view the risk around late stage development and construction.

in regards to the current portfolio in the US, I have disregarded an important component - about a fourth (250MW) of capacity is under PPA's that are being renegotiated. If there ever is a perfect moment to be long clean baseload electricity, this might be it. These contracts often go out 10 or more years (as they anchor construction of new infrastructure) and I expect strong pricing and upside to recurring EBITDA (for competitive reasons there is little available to model this, so it is just that - upside).

Recent earnings:

Ormat released Q1 2025 earnings on May 7th. All metrics are pointing toward continues progress toward the stated EBITDA goal. At $150 million in Q1, that would annualize to $600 million, well ahead of last year (and several projects will CoD later this year).

Stellar growth from BESS revenues (+120%) segment is likely to continue, as these deployments scale, so will the related margins, which improved to 30.6% form 7.5% in the Q1 of last year (in Q1 2025, margins were also helped by strong pricing on merchant markets within PJM during winter months).

Going forward, key milestones to watch are around the progress of development milestones, especially the four geothermal projects expected to come online in the remaining part of 2025:

What can go wrong

Well, as noted, debt is not a concern for this independent power producer. The cash flows are highly contracted, and the offtakers are usually investment grade US utilities (like NV Energy, a Berkshire Hathaway Energy company).

With new projects, there is always risk of cost overruns. In early stage developments, the risk of “dry wells” is a recurring possibility (the development pipeline is not dependent on discoveries). Those risks – exploration - are often shared with other parties, like offtakers. The current rush to secure clean power for data centers is giving more leverage to IPPs to negotiate risk sharing mechanisms as well.

The technology has developed slowly for a reason. The high cost of development, due to drilling and exploration is some 50% higher than a combined cycle gas plant (which is efficient and cleaner but not emission free). Geo power plants are economical in high marginal cost systems (emerging markets lacking domestic energy reserves) or with the aid of incentives (in the US).

The world, including the DOE, has been moving decisively toward incentivizing renewables, and that is not expected to change, although more emphasis should be placed on baseload solutions like geothermal.

Like all renewables, you have to go where the resource is. In this case, the resource is found near fault lines of major tectonic plates. That means, similar to wind and solar, transmission infrastructure needs to be put in place. Near term, most of the development pipeline is within existing infrastructure in Nevada and California. Longer term, this limits potential and requieres national policies to align. New Zealand and Indonesia are the main growth opportunities outside the US (Ormat will likely participate mostly as a supplier of equipment and services).

Finally, international clients expose the company to higher credit risk and some FX risk as well. The size of the international footprint is not large (and electricity sales can usually be redirected to another client should an offtaker default).

Conclusion:

Geothermal is almost purpose-built for data centers and hyperscale demand. They need continuous clean power, so much so, that are willing to backstop small nuclear projects or re-start old ones. Geothermal is easier an cheaper than both of those.

Ormat, the worlds leading operator (and equipment supplier), is well positioned in this "niche" technology. Although significant cost reductions and deeper drilling will gradually help bring the technology closer to main-stream, the investment thesis is not dependent on that.

The company currently has very high visibility on reaching $800 million EBITDA. In addition, that EBITDA may have embedded upside from PPA negotiations that are ongoing for about 1/4th of the US generation capacity. Given modest valuation scenarios of around 12x EBITDA, which is lower than prevailing, and ignoring potential upside from PPA renegotiation, there is still about 50% stock appreciation is in the cards by 2028.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cisco: Low Growth Despite AI Infrastructure Opportunities
data.description:
Cisco Systems, Inc. (NASDAQ:CSCO) reported Q3 2025 earnings that beat Wall Street estimates on revenues and earnings per share. I used to own a small number of shares in Cisco, and the stock had done well for me when it was part of my portfolio, but it was not necessarily impressive. It was more of a name that steadily grew. That also should not be a major surprise given Cisco’s positioning in the market.

In this report, I discuss the company’s most recent earnings, the outlook for Q4 and FY25, and I apply a stock price target to the name.

Cisco Positioned For Support Of AI Hyperscalers

Cisco is not a company that is going to see huge AI growth. That is driven by its significant exposure to the more traditional product line up of switches and routers. That does position the company to see some AI growth from supporting hyperscalers, but the company is not a provider of foundational models. Its AI growth comes more from providing AI infrastructure with some AI-powered analytics and security applications. So, the company can grow its revenues driven by demand from networking solutions for cloud and AI companies.

The company is working with Nvidia (NVDA) to build systems that use Nvidia hardware and Cisco software for use in data centers. The company also has a broader effort to tailor its hardware for AI workload needs. In my view, Cisco is not an AI company in the traditional sense. The company just provides networking solutions in support of AI companies. That does not mean that the company cannot benefit from AI growth, as AI capital expenditures drive networking hardware and software demand and AI is infused in threat detection for the security business, network optimization and telemetry.

Cisco Earnings Beat as Security and Observability Drive Growth

In the third quarter, sales grew 11% to $14.1 billion, beating analyst estimates by $91.5 million. Product sales grew 15% year-on-year while services revenues grew 2.7% year-on-year. Product revenues grew 54%, driven by growth in security while observability sales were up 24%, networking sales were up 8% and collaboration sales were up 4%. The surge in product revenues was largely driven by the acquisition of Splunk which was completed in March 2024.

Margins grew modestly by 30 basis points to 68.6%, and that translated through to the operating income margins, which also grew 30 basis points resulting in a operating come increase slightly above the top-line growth. Core earnings per share of $0.96 beat estimates by $0.04.

Cisco Has A Strong Track Record Of Beating Wall Street Estimates

For the full year, the company expects $56.5 billion to $56.7 billion in sales compared to analyst estimates that have expectations set at the lower end of the range. EPS of $3.77-$3.79 would indicate little year-on-year growth with analysts having set their expectations at the high end of the range. For Q4, Cisco expects sales between $14.5 billion and $14.7 billion pointing at 8% year-on-year growth at the mid-point of the range while earnings per share are expected to be between $0.96 and $0.98. Analysts once again are expecting earnings to come in at the high end of the range.

I don’t believe it provides a substantial risk that analysts have their expectations for EPS set at the high end of the range. The reason is quite simple: Cisco seemingly always beats estimates.

Is Cisco Stock Attractive?

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

While Cisco can definitely capitalize on AI infrastructure demand, it is not expected to be extremely high growth. The CAGR for EBITDA growth towards FY27 is a mere 3.3%. Free cash flow is likely going to be significantly better at 19.9% annually. However, that does not get us much further than a hold rating and a $65.77 price target. This provides 7.7% upside, which is below the returns I am looking for. As a result, I am marking the stock a hold.

Conclusion: Slow Growth Company

Cisco is not necessarily unattractive from earnings potential point of view, and it is working on making its products better suited for AI use. However, if we connect that to the current valuation, it is more of a low growth company rather than a major AI play and that is more than fine, but also means I am rating CSCO stock a Hold.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Walmart Rings Warning Bell On Tariffs
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

WMT execs say suppliers and retailers can’t absorb all the higher costs. (0:15) Wholesale inflaton sinks unexpectedly. (1:11) Happy 85th birthday, McDonald’s! (4:03)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, Walmart (NYSE:WMT) management warned during their earnings call and in interviews that tariffs could lead to higher prices for U.S. consumers.

CFO John David Rainey said: "We’re wired for everyday low prices, but the magnitude of these increases is more than any retailer can absorb. It’s more than any supplier can absorb. And so I’m concerned that consumer is going to start seeing higher prices. You’ll begin to see that, likely towards the tail end of this month, and then certainly much more in June."

CEO Doug McMillon said the company will do its best to keep consumer prices as low as possible, but due to the magnitude of the tariffs, even at the reduced levels announced this week, the company can't absorb all the pressure given the reality of narrow retail margins.

Walmart held off on issuing Q2 EPS and operating income guidance with its report.

The warning on prices wiped out premarket gains seen after Walmart released its Q1 results. Comparable sales in the U.S. rose 4.5% to top the consensus estimate of 3.9%.

It was a busy morning on the macro front

Wholesale inflation fell unexpectedly in April, with the Producer Price Index down -0.5%, compared with the +0.2% expected and flat in March (revised from -0.4%). The annual rate fell to +2.4% from +3.4% prior (revised from +2.7%). That was lower than then +2.5% consensus.

Over 40% of the April decline in the index for final demand services is primarily due to margins for machinery and vehicle wholesaling, which fell 6.1%.

Economist Joseph Brusuelas says: “This is evidence that trade policy is resulting in margin compression, which will then result in consumers absorbing higher prices starting in May.”

Ex food and energy, the core PPI fell -0.4% M/M, as compared to +0.3% consensus, and +0.4% prior (revised from -0.1%). On a yearly basis, it’s up +3.1%, meeting consensus.

Pantheon Macro economists say “CPI and PPI data imply the core PCE deflator rose by 0.12% in April, reducing the inflation rate to 2.5%, from 2.6% in March.”

In addition, retail sales edged up +0.1% M/M in April, a hair above the no change expected and decelerating from the prior month's 1.7% increase (revised from +1.4%),

Excluding motor vehicles and parts, core retail sales also inched up +0.1% from a month earlier, missing the +0.3% consensus and slowing from +0.8% in March (revised from +0.5%).

Janney strategist Guy LeBas says while “retail sales were ‘okay,’ they don't paint a picture of a consumer rushing to buy ahead of tariffs. If anything, retail sales show subtle signs of belt-tightening ahead of employment risks in coming months.”

Wells Fargo economists says: “A key question after this week’s 90-day reprieve from a full-blown trade war with China is whether the sharp lowering in the tariff rate for one of America’s largest trading partners comes quickly enough to blunt the worst of the economic fallout.”

“If tariffs indeed introduced the ‘stagflationary shock’ we have warned about, then an unwinding of those tariffs surely has scope to boost growth and lower inflation, if not in an absolute sense, at least relative to expectations that priced in tariff-induced shocks.”

On top of all that, Fed Chairman Jerome Powell spoke, but kept things focused on its framework review that it embarks on every five years. This time around, communications will be an important focus, chiefly how to convey the degree of uncertainty facing the economy.

Powell did note: “Higher real rates may also reflect the possibility that inflation could be more volatile going forward than in the inter-crisis period of the 2010s. We may be entering a period of more frequent, and potentially more persistent, supply shocks — a difficult challenge for the economy and for central banks.”

In other news of note, McDonald's (MCD) turns 85 years old today. The original McDonald's restaurant was opened by Richard and Maurice McDonald in San Bernardino, California in 1940. The restaurant’s "Speedee Service System" attracted Ray Kroc, who joined as a franchising agent in 1954 and opened the first franchised McDonald’s in Des Plaines, Illinois, in 1955 before buying out the brothers in 1961. By the 1970s, McDonald’s had become a global icon, introducing signature menu items and expanding into international markets.

Looking ahead, McDonald's plans to expand, aiming for over 50,000 locations by 2027. Near term menu innovations include a continuation of the $5 Meal Deal, expansion of Big Arch Burgers, and the McCrispy chicken sandwich and strips going international.

Shares of McDonald's (MCD) have outperformed the S&P 500 Index since 1995 by more than 600 percentage points

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, BMO Capital Markets is taking a victory lap, keeping its S&P 500 year-end target at 6,100 and emphasizing a commitment to a disciplined investment process rather than reacting to market fluctuations.

Strategist Brian Belski says: “Our bullish stance on U.S. stocks has been belittled, doubted and ridiculed so far in 2025 like no other time in our career.”

“Demand for U.S. stocks is and should be robust – period,” he said, adding that this resilience indicates the market is appropriately rewarding quality assets.

“Yes, we revised our year-end forecast in terms of price and earnings just five weeks ago,” Belski said. “However, to clarify, we did maintain our initial 2025 forecast (6,700 + $275) as our bull case for the remainder of the year. So why did we revise lower? Discretion is the better part of valor, and a 30%-plus move higher in the face of profoundly negative momentum, coupled with equally volatile exogenous news flow was a far from prudent recipe for investing.”

Looking ahead, he recommends watching stabilization of earnings alongside returning company guidance, less reactive price movements to Washington news and continued broadly distributed equity performance as signposts for market stability.

data.title: Dr. Reddy's Continues To Be A Good Buy At Current Valuation
data.description:
Introduction

I last wrote about Dr. Reddy’s Laboratories (NYSE:RDY) in January. At that time, I initiated my coverage on RDY with a strong buy rating. The rating stemmed from a very lucrative valuation that RDY’s stock was trading at. The cheap valuation was there for a reason, like the potential revenue drop-off from the patent expiration of Revlimid. However, I believed that this headwind has been exaggerated by the market. Moreover, the market is also not appreciating RDY’s initiatives in the high-growth biosimilar and GLP-1 segments.

Since my initial rating on the stock, the stock price has largely remained where it was three months ago. The stock has returned ~3% as compared to the S&P 500 declining 2% for the same period. I believe the thesis is still intact with the recent upbeat results.

Upbeat results

Earlier in May, RDY came out with upbeat numbers exceeding consensus estimates on both the top and bottom lines. The company reported revenue of ~$996 million, growing 20% YoY, while GAAP EPS came in at $0.22 per share, beating the consensus by ~$0.02.

While topline growth looks quite robust, much of this growth is attributed to the acquisition of Nicotine Replacement therapy (NRT) business and contribution from the generic portfolio. The acquired NRT business contributed ~$70 million in 4Q; excluding the benefit from this acquisition, the growth should have been ~12% YoY.

On a segmental basis, the company reported ~23% growth in the Global generic business. Within this, the majority of the growth came from the Europe business, which grew 142% YoY, thanks to the NRT acquisition. The Indian business grew ~16% YoY due to successful launches of 23 new brands in FY25, exclusive of the Sanofi and Nestle portfolio. Furthermore, existing brands also contributed considerably by growing double digits for the quarter. As far as North America is concerned, the company was able to report good growth even though there were significant headwinds in the US market with respect to price erosion. The company managed to deliver 7% YoY growth in the North American market, driven by increased volume of key products such as Revlimid and new product launches.

Other than the global generic segment the Pharmaceutical and Active Ingredients (PSAI) segment grew 13% YoY, primarily due to new API launches and growth in CDMO business.

With respect to profitability, the company reported 55.6% gross margins, a ~300 bps decline YoY, due to price erosion in the North American market as well as higher outlicensing income recognized during the prior period. Moreover, severance costs related to the divestment of the Freeport plant also led to a one-time higher cost.

SG&A expense also rose ~17% YoY, albeit slower than the revenue growth, leading to faster growth in the EBITDA, which grew ~32% YoY.

Outlook

Going down the line, RDY is set to witness a major fall in revenue relating to the IP expiration of Revlimid. Revlimid has been a considerable contributor to RDY profitability since it reached the agreement with Celegene to sell the generic version of Revlimid. Since the drug’s patent is set to expire in Jan 2026, the profits from this drug would taper off starting from FY27.

While the expected decrease in Revlimid sales is expected to impact RDY significantly, the company has strategically positioned itself to weather this headwind by allocating cash towards future product pipelines in high-growth markets. These high-growth markets include the peptide, biosimilar and GLP-1 markets, where the company has ~26 meaningful new products in the pipeline, which are expected to launch in the next 2-3 years. Specific to these new products, the most important of these products are generic Semaglutide (GLP-1), Rituximab, Denosumab, and Abatacept (biosimilars). Each of these products could be a multi-million dollar opportunity for RDY upon commercialization.

If we go by sell-side estimates, the crosswinds of both the decline in revenue from Revlimid and the tailwind from new product launches are effectively going to negate each other, as FY27 topline growth projections point to just a 1% YoY decline.

Although profitability would take a hit initially, I believe this should soon rebound as the new product matures and profitability rises. This can also be seen in the sell-side estimates, where EPS initially falls in FY27 (when the patent expires) and then soon rebounds.

Tariff Uncertainties

The return of President Donald Trump and his subsequent tariff wars with various counterparts around the world has sparked debate about whether generic imported pharmaceuticals should also be included in Trump’s tariff list.

Till now the generic drugs imported from India have so far been untouched. even when the US announced 27% reciprocal tariffs on India, the pharmaceutical exports were intentionally exempted from those tariffs. While president Trump has occasionally stated he would put tariffs on pharmaceutical imports, I believe it is highly unlikely to materialize, primarily due to the cost burden it would put on American patients.

As of now, ~90% of the US generic drugs are imported from India and China. The major import dependence is primarily due to the lower cost base of India and China, which is important for generic drugs, as they are off-patent drugs that have a highly competitive market. These drugs benefit the patients by offering drugs that are off patent at affordable rates. Imposing tariffs on these drugs would simply mean a higher cost structure for producers based outside the US. As most generic drugs are imported, it would mean a price increase of drugs equivalent to the tariffs imposed.

While tariffs could result in a rise in domestic manufacturing, I believe a significant and sustained tariff barrier would be needed for the domestic manufacturers to be competitive from the asian counterparts. Moreover, setting up a manufacturing facility would take quite long and even longer for the supply chain to move to the US. Moreover, if ever a significant and sustained tariff barrier is imposed, RDY also has the option to establish its own manufacturing facility in the USA to cater to the domestic market. However, I still believe the scenario of a very high and sustained tariff on pharmaceuticals to be highly unlikely given the political backlash this measure could face.

Valuation and Conclusion

RDY is currently trading at ~18x Non-GAAP forward PE ratio, which is in line with the sector median but lower than the 5-year average of 22.6x. While on the surface the PE multiples might look a little elevated, if we factor in the high historical and potential growth rate of Dr. Reddy, the multiple looks justified.

Moreover, when compared to the peers, the company looks quite cheap.

Given RDY’s cheap valuation, I believe the market is concerned regarding the patent expiration of Revlimid. However, the market is also underappreciating the investment that the company has been doing in other high-growth markets, which are soon going to realize and partially reduce the impact of Revlimid. Given this underappreciated potential of RDY and cheap valuation, I am reiterating the strong buy rating on the stock.

data.title: Silver ETFs: Shining Through The Market Noise
data.description:
In 2025, we have had to go back to the basics, including diversification. One of the most well-known ways to diversify has been with commodities. Commodities have low correlation to other asset classes, including equities and fixed income. While gold has been the commodity favorite for many investors, silver has started to receive more attention recently as signals point to possible undervaluation. Investors can access silver through physical silver ETFs or silver mining equity ETFs.

While gold often outshines silver, silver has some unique benefits

Like gold, silver is a precious metal that serves as a “store of value.” Its supply is somewhat limited, which makes it valuable. But compared to gold, there is a significant difference. As of May 12, gold costs around $3,200 per troy ounce, while silver costs around $30. But unlike gold, silver comes with more industrial uses. Silver has high conductivity, which makes it suitable for use in vehicles, machinery, and electronics. This makes it as much of an investment opportunity as it does a method of savings. It also adds an extra layer of risk. For example, if industrial demand goes down due to tariffs or other economic/geopolitical conditions, that could affect demand for silver.

While silver often moves alongside gold, a well-followed ratio is the gold-to-silver ratio. That is the cost of gold per ounce/cost of silver per ounce. This ratio has recently climbed and is currently hovering at around 100. A higher ratio (at least higher than 80) indicates silver may be undervalued relative to gold.

Investors often choose physical silver ETFs as a portfolio diversifier

There are only two ETFs that solely hold physical silver. The oldest and largest fund is the iShares Silver Trust (SLV), which has $14.7 billion in assets. For reference, the largest gold ETF, the SPDR Gold Shares (GLD), has $100.2 billion in assets. So there is a significant gap in size between the two. The abrdn Physical Silver Shares ETF (SIVR) is much smaller, at $1.6 billion. But it comes with a lower fee (30 basis points compared to SLV’s 50 basis points).

Some investors may also choose short-term exposure to silver through leveraged silver ETFs. Investors can target 2x daily exposure to silver through the ProShares Ultra Silver (AGQ) and -2x daily exposure through the ProShares UltraShort Silver (ZSL). Both funds use derivatives (e.g., futures contracts and swaps) to replicate performance. For a deeper look at the leveraged ETF universe, see this note.

While less popular than pure silver exposure, silver miners may offer additional returns

Outside of investing in physical silver ETFs, investors can also access silver through ETFs of silver mining equities. These add an extra layer of equity risk, but may offer higher returns when the underlying commodity (silver) performs well. Currently, silver mining ETFs are up around 25% YTD, while physical silver ETFs are up around 12%.

  • The largest ETF in the space is the Global X Silver Miners ETF (SIL), which was launched in 2010. It currently has $1.6 billion in assets. This ETF looks at global silver miners and is weighted by market capitalization. Like many market-cap-weighted ETFs, this ETF is top-heavy, with the first two holdings totaling to 35% of its weight. Its top holding is Wheaton Precious Metals Corp. (WPM, WPM:CA), which has been up 37% YTD.
  • The Amplify Junior Silver Miners ETF (SILJ) is the first and only ETF that looks at “junior miners.” which are small-cap silver miners. It does, however, have significant crossover with its peers. It uses a “theme-adjusted” market-cap weighting system. That includes market cap, silver revenue percentage, and liquidity. As a result, stock weights are relatively diversified. Top holdings include Coeur Mining (CDE) and First Majestic Silver Corp. (AG). Compared to its peers, the junior silver mining space is only slightly underperforming this year (22% vs. 25%).
  • The iShares MSCI Global Silver and Metal Miners ETF (SLVP) combines silver miners with other metal miners. While this explicitly states that in the name, all of its peers more or less also contain other miners. (And most miners derive revenue from several different metals so lines are often blurred.) Its top three holdings are 41% of the ETF’s weight, including 20% in its top holding, Pan American Silver (PAAS, PAAS:CA).
  • The Sprott Silver Miners & Physical Silver ETF (SLVR) was launched in January by Sprott. This issuer is a well-known manager in the metals and materials space. SLVR has a large allocation to physical silver through a Sprott trust (approximately 17%). It is the only ETF of the group to combine miners with physical silver. Along with SLVP, this has the most exposure to silver miners over other metal miners (classified by GICS). SLVR also has the least overlap with its peers in terms of its top 10 holdings.
  • The Sprott Active Gold & Silver Miners ETF (GBUG) launched in February. This is the only active ETF of the group. It combines both gold and silver miners with the thesis that miners may be undervalued versus bullion and that an active strategy has the flexibility to take advantage of those lower valuations. Currently, GBUG has mostly gold miners and is among the most diversified when it comes to individual holdings. Its top holding is only around 5% of the ETF’s weight. This strategy may be more popular for investors who want a more diversified approach to metals miners instead of focusing separately on silver miners. As mentioned earlier, it’s often hard to target silver miners over gold miners. That’s because many mining companies have diversified revenue streams.
  • The Themes Silver Miners ETF (AGMI) was launched last year. Like many of Themes’ products, AGMI has an expense ratio on the lower end of its peers. This ETF is also among the most diversified globally (although still leaning heavily toward Canada).

Disclosure: © VettaFi LLC 2024. All rights reserved. This material has been prepared and/or issued by VettaFi LLC ("VettaFi") and/or one of its consultants or affiliates. It is provided as general information only and should not be taken as investment advice. Employees of VettaFi are prohibited from owning individual MLPs. For more information on VettaFi, visit VettaFi.

data.title: International Container Terminal: Still Bullish Considering Tariff Developments And Q1 Outperformance
data.description:
I'm a bull on International Container Terminal Services (OTCPK:ICTEF) (ICT.PS) or "ICTS".

The prior January 27, 2025 write-up touched on ICTS's outlook in the Asian and Americas markets.

My opinion of ICTS as an investment is still positive. I have looked at the company's tariff-related risks and its first quarter financials in making this judgment.

I Worry Less About Tariff War After Assessing Market Events And Corporate Disclosures

Going forward, any valuation discount assigned to ICTS due to "Liberation Day tariffs" should narrow.

It's natural to think that port operator ICTS will be hurt by trade volume contraction resulting from the ongoing tariff war. But recent developments have given me the conviction that ICTS's future performance will be resilient.

The United States and PRC (People's Republic of China) have reached a "truce" this week. In response, Goldman Sachs (GS) lifted its '25 GDP expansion estimates for the former and the latter by 50 bps (basis points) and 0.6 ppts (percentage points), respectively. I obtained these figures from GS's May 14 research note (not accessible publicly) titled "Tariffs Turnover".

In early-May, a maiden "post-Liberation Day trade deal" was inked between the U.K. and America. A May 10 Bloomberg report cited sources suggesting that the U.S. is prioritizing conversations with selected countries to resolve trade differences.

I would think that the Sino-US tariff war pause marks the beginning of de-escalation, which will be good for ICTS.

Separately, ICTS provided clarifications about its risk exposure at its Q1'25 analyst call (S&P Capital IQ transcript) on May 5.

ICTS indicated that a modest, low-single digit percentage of its total volumes are directly linked to U.S.-Chinese trades. Even if the two superpowers re-initiate a tariff war later, the impact on ICTS should be limited.

Also, the company shared at the first quarter results briefing that it didn't witness "front-loading" activities for its terminals in the initial four months of 2025. This supports my favorable view of ICTS's portfolio composition.

In the late-January piece, I noted that ICTS's assets are mostly "focused on origin & departure that cater to the host countries' needs", as opposed to "trade-sensitive trans-shipments".

My take is the stock's earnings multiple should expand. One catalyst is an improvement in the global trade environment. The other re-rating trigger is investors' gradual realization that the group isn't as tariff-exposed as initially feared.

ICTS's Solid Q1 Growth Points To A Bright Future

ICTS saw its EBITDA go up by an impressive +19% YoY (Year-on-Year) to $0.49B in Q1'25.

The company benefited from multiple profit levers.

For the first three months of '25, ICTS's throughput went up by 12% to 3.5M TEUs (Twenty-Foot Equivalent Units) on a year-on-year basis.

My Jan-25 article predicted that ICTS's Americas operations will return to positive growth because it signed "a deal in Q4'24 to secure commitments from new carriers for (its Ecuadorian port) CGSA." I was proven to be right. This geographic segment achieved a +14% higher volume for Q1 2025, which is better than the group-level low-teens rise.

Its yield/TEU expanded by +4% to $209 for the latest quarter in YoY terms.

The roll-out of new high-margin services (complementary to core port operations), and a mix shift with a bigger share of more profitable container shipments, boosted ICTS's Q1 returns.

Also, EBITDA margin for ICTS widened by +1 ppt to 66% in Q1'25 thanks to fixed-cost leverage effects.

Looking forward, I'm projecting that ICTS can at least register a low-teens percentage increase in EBITDA in both FY2025 and FY26.

I think that some of the Q1 earnings drivers will continue to have a positive effect on the group in the quarters ahead. These include the financial improvement for the terminal in Ecuador, and additional ancillary revenue streams.

Further upside could come from price hikes and inorganic growth initiatives.

ICTS has no less than five (source: analyst briefing) M&A targets in its pipeline. These potential acquirees are operating in Africa/ASEAN/Latin-America.

Its Mexican asset is due for a rate review in the following year. The company's domestic (Philippines) port had already raised its pricing by 600bps in Q1, and this should have a favorable full-year impact.

Bear-Case Scenarios You Should Think About

A worldwide recession triggered by a full-blown trade war will mean that even local economies get hit. In that case, ICTS's O&D (Origin & Departure) ports will also be vulnerable and suffer from weaker demand.

There is the possibility of the company reporting disappointing results. ICTS might fail to conclude its planned M&As. It could also face challenges in adjusting prices upwards or gaining client acceptance for new offerings.

The Final Word

ICTS is still worthy of a Buy rating.

Its tariff-related risks are not that substantial, considering the latest events and management clarifications.

I anticipate that the group will build on its strong Q1'25 results and perform similarly well in the future.

The stock is cheap based on peer and fundamental comparisons. ICTS is now trading at 9x NTM (Next Twelve Months) EV/EBITDA.

The average EBITDA multiple for Asia-listed port businesses Westports (OTCPK:WSTTF) and Adani Ports (APSE.NS) was more demanding at 13 times as per S&P Capital IQ data.

The group's historical (Q1'25) and expected (FY25/26) EBITDA growth rates are in the teens percent range. In my view, this implies that ICTS's EV-EBITDA ratio should also be above 10 in alignment with its operating profit metrics.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Dark Side Of Infrastructure Stock Investing
data.description:
I love investing in infrastructure stocks. In fact, it is my favorite sector in general to invest in right now. That said, there is also a dark side to investing in the sector that investors need to be wary of. In this article, I will discuss why I like investing in infrastructure so much, while also providing a warning against the biggest pitfalls that befall investors in this space.

Why I Love Infrastructure Investing

The biggest reason why I love investing in infrastructure is that businesses in this sector tend to provide a very attractive combination of highly predictable cash flows due to the fact that most infrastructure businesses have either contracted or regulated assets that make their cash flows almost bond-like in nature.

Infrastructure also tends to be very inflation-resistant, since most contracts and regulatory agreements have either inflation-linked escalators or at the very least inflation protections in them. On top of that, infrastructure is generally made up of real assets, which tend to appreciate with inflation over time due to increased replacement costs and the fact that some of it is mission-critical in nature and therefore cannot be easily replaced—if replaced at all.

Another reason why I really like investing in infrastructure businesses is because of the highly predictable cash flow streams they generate. They also tend to pay out high dividends. This is great for me as a value investor because I have a much easier time valuing these companies since I can count on the dividend stream being a stable source of returns and then can simply factor in more predictable growth rates on top of that, instead of worrying about how well management is going to be managing my capital and wondering if the numbers they were publishing were fudged or not. This is especially important in a real asset business where GAAP accounting metrics like earnings per share are relatively meaningless due to depreciation, which can, in turn, make it very hard to understand a company's financials. That makes paying out the stable and growing dividends over time that good infrastructure companies do consistently well an important safeguard that proves management is not fudging the numbers too much.

Another reason I really like investing in infrastructure right now is because it is benefiting from some major macro tailwinds: the need to dramatically modernize and upgrade infrastructure in much of the developed world, including the United States; the need for massive amounts of new essential infrastructure across the developing world due to rapid population and economic growth and development; and the large demand for infrastructure-type assets by alternative asset managers like BlackRock (BLK) and Brookfield (BN)(BAM) due to the need for the stable yielding cash flows that it provides to fund retirement pensions—with a large number of people in developed countries hitting retirement.

On top of that, there is incredible demand for energy infrastructure right now—particularly clean and inexpensive energy—due to continued demand to reduce carbon emissions as well as build out a large, reliable power base to support the ongoing AI boom. Hyperscalers like Microsoft (MSFT) are doling out huge power purchase agreements to the likes of Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP)(BEPC) to deliver clean and reliable energy to power their AI ambitions.

Related to that, there is also a huge amount of demand for digital infrastructure, particularly data centers, as hyperscalers—and governments—are investing aggressively in building out the computational ability to win the AI race.

The Dark Side Of Infrastructure Investing

While all of these are highly compelling reasons to invest in infrastructure—and in fact, this is why infrastructure is my favorite sector to invest in—as with any sector, it is not a guaranteed slam dunk, and there are some serious cons to investing in infrastructure stocks. Here are several aspects of the dark side.

First of all, when investing in infrastructure stocks, it is very important to insist on balance sheet strength. Just because a company has an investment-grade credit rating does not mean that it has a strong balance sheet, either. For example, Algonquin Power & Utilities (AQN) had a BBB credit rating, yet slashed its dividend twice—and very deeply each time—in a short period of time due to having a weak balance sheet that apparently the ratings agencies did not fully capture.

This was also the case with Energy Transfer (ET), which had an investment-grade balance sheet heading into the COVID energy crash, but due to overly ambitious capex, significant weakness in energy prices, and rapidly declining capital market access for hydrocarbon companies in that brief window of time, ET felt pressure to slash its distribution in half in order to shore up its investment-grade credit rating. Additionally, NextEra Energy Partners —now known as XPLR Infrastructure (XIFR)—is an infrastructure business that was growing its distribution rapidly and seemed to be a wonderful company with a great track record. Yet its balance sheet became extremely overleveraged with all sorts of complicated and opaque means of financing, which ultimately forced it to completely eliminate its distribution, and its unit price plummeted in response.

While it is true that prudent financing of assets—including the use of leverage—can dramatically enhance returns in infrastructure investing (something that asset managers like Brookfield do exceptionally well with PIKs through its funds as well as its subsidiaries such as BEP and Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIPC)), the prudence of the structuring is extremely important. If the company is overleveraged or even simply fails to structure its leverage in a conservative manner, it can completely blow up the investment thesis, leading to steep dividend cuts and severe capital losses for shareholders.

Another important aspect to keep in mind is regulatory and geopolitical challenges. For example, if you have a regulated asset such as a utility business, you are largely at the mercy of the jurisdiction’s rate cases to determine if you are going to have profitability growth over time. As a result, if you end up in an unfavorable jurisdiction, your business can end up offering a very poor risk-reward because you have equity exposure to the stock—which exposes you to massive downside risk in the event of poor balance sheet management, as is the case with AQN—or even if a natural disaster strikes, like what recently happened to Hawaiian Electric (HE), you can get crushed. Meanwhile, your upside ends up not being great due to unfavorable rate rulings that can lead to, at best, bond-like returns.

Another con to keep in mind is that sectors such as hydrocarbon energy infrastructure can be very politically and regulatorily sensitive because these businesses often depend on favorable granting of permits and government policies in order to get permission to even build their projects to grow their businesses. The same goes for renewable energy infrastructure, as, in addition to permitting, in some cases renewables still depend on government subsidies to be competitive.

Additionally, when you are investing in transportation infrastructure, you have to deal with potential disruption to trade. This can come from a pandemic, like we saw with COVID-19, or even simply trade wars like we recently saw with the U.S.–China trade war that led to a massive drop in trade between the two countries, even if for just a short period of time.

Finally, when investing in foreign countries, you also have the risk of a lack of respect for private property. For example, in Mali recently, Barrick Gold Corporation’s (B) mining infrastructure has been tampered with and potentially may be seized by the Malian government—leading to a likely permanent write-down for shareholders if that event plays out.

This is what makes investing in third-world infrastructure projects so risky—yet potentially lucrative—because there is not as much competition for them due to the risks, and often the upside is exceptional if management can successfully navigate the region. This is why focusing on good management teams that have strong relationships in those countries and proven experience operating there is essential when investing in international infrastructure opportunities.

Another risk to keep in mind is currency headwinds, especially when investing in international infrastructure investments. For example, in BIP's latest quarter, there was a significant gap between their headline growth rate and their growth rate adjusted for forex volatility. This is despite the fact that Brookfield tends to hedge considerably against foreign exchange in many of its investments.

Another example of this is Patria Investments (PAX), which is a rapidly growing alternative asset manager focused on Latin America. Despite consistently meeting and beating its guidance in delivering phenomenal growth while paying out an attractive dividend, the stock has languished behind American-based peers—in large part due to weak currency performance in Latin America relative to the U.S. dollar over the past years. Of course, this can cut both ways—if the dollar does depreciate, as it has some in recent months due to trade uncertainty and tariffs, this can make these international investments even more attractive than they otherwise would be. However, it still adds uncertainty and volatility to the investment, which makes international investments all the more risky. While this does not mean that they should be avoided, it is definitely another aspect to keep in mind when investing to ensure that you are maintaining proper diversification across your portfolio.

A final major risk to keep in mind is getting caught up in market irrational exuberance. This can often be the case with infrastructure, simply because the story is so attractive and exciting that the market often gets ahead of itself when it comes to infrastructure growth stories and can bid up valuations too high. It is therefore important to maintain proper value investor discipline when buying these stocks—instead of chasing them higher—by waiting for good opportunities to buy them.

We saw this with BEP shortly after Joe Biden and the Democrats took power in early 2021 following the U.S. election, which caused many to become extremely bullish on renewable power—especially given that interest rates were quite low at the time. This led to massive underperformance for people who bought BEP near the top, as they may have gotten caught up in the hype, and renewable power did not end up experiencing quite the boom that was expected. Meanwhile, interest rates rose rapidly due to soaring inflation, which greatly hurt that capital-intensive industry.

Another example that is even more recent is Blackstone (BX), which became significantly overvalued due to market bullishness on infrastructure growth potential across the world, including the AI boom as well as optimism surrounding U.S.–China trade—which, of course, has come crashing down recently, and this has hurt BX shareholders as well.

Investor Takeaway

As this article points out, infrastructure investing has a lot going for it, both in terms of qualitative factors as well as broader macro tailwinds. If you can therefore focus on quality assets with prudent balance sheet management and skilled management teams that know what they are doing in their specific infrastructure segment as well as geographic region—and can then be disciplined in only buying shares when they trade at fair or better prices—you can do extremely well over the long term, especially on a risk-adjusted basis.

However, you also need to be mindful of the significant risks that come with infrastructure investing. If you can keep these in mind and mitigate them as best you can through both careful due diligence as well as adequate diversification, you can avoid the dark side of infrastructure investing and achieve rich, long-term total return and dividend growth results. This is what we have been able to do thus far with great success at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Global Atomic: Great Future For Uranium And Zinc With Electrification And Energy Diversification
data.description:
A “Buy” Rating for Global Atomic Corp.

In our view, investors who take a long position in publicly traded shares of uranium producers, or developers such as Global Atomic Corp. (OTCQX:GLATF)'s OTCQX-listed shares, could be making a smart move.

The company's portfolio consists of two business units, as Global Atomic Corp. explains in its company profile:

“The Company's Uranium Division is currently developing the fully permitted, large, high grade Dasa Deposit, discovered in 2010 […] and commissioning of the processing plant is scheduled for Q2 2026. Global Atomic has also identified 3 additional uranium deposits in Niger that will be advanced with further assessment work.

Global Atomic's Base Metals Division holds a 49% interest in the Befesa Silvermet Turkey, S.L. (BST) Joint Venture, which operates a modern zinc recycling plant, located in Iskenderun, Türkiye. The plant recovers zinc from Electric Arc Furnace Dust (EAFD) to produce a high-grade zinc oxide concentrate which is sold to zinc smelters around the world […].”

Uranium Division

Uranium is now the most important nuclear fuel. It is mainly used for electricity generation in nuclear power plants, which implies a low and decreasing risk of accidents. There is also a lower probability of major accidents and the radiological effects on humans can be prevented. Nuclear power plants produce clean energy compared to the combustion of fossil fuels and, together with other energy sources, can help meet the global demand for electricity, which is increasing rapidly with the proliferation and development of data centers equipped with artificial intelligence software.

The spread of nuclear power generation technologies – i.e., the construction of new reactors and the extension of reactor operating life – will be the main driver of uranium demand in the long term.

In our view, the hype surrounding AI-powered data centers will not be the real driver of growth, although more reactors will undoubtedly be needed to meet the world's growing energy demand. Rather, the geopolitical factor will play a role. If a country's way of life is too dependent on a particular energy source or supplier, families and businesses will pay a high price for speculation when it arises, like the one that triggered the energy crisis in the spring/summer of 2022 after Russia invaded Ukraine. To avoid dependence on a single energy source or supplier, countries' strategies to diversify their energy sources are crucial. Since these plans cannot be fully implemented unless uranium-based nuclear energy is also included, backed by a reasonable expectation of new reactors coming online, we can optimistically predict a higher demand for uranium, which may take as long as necessary, but will eventually be moving up over time, thus putting upward pressure on prices per pound.

It is no coincidence that the slope of the LT trend in uranium prices changed decisively to positive after the energy crisis and the outbreak of war in Ukraine, as awareness of the importance of diversifying energy sources grew among households and businesses in wealthy Europe.

Uranium Price Forecasts

Uranium prices are likely to be higher in a few years, which would not only bode well for, but also reinforce, the bullish sentiment toward Global Atomic Corp. stock, as the company is in the process of developing its 80 percent stake in the Dasa project in Niger into a uranium mine and some analysts are comfortable with the forecasts for the uranium price per pound.

“Bank of America remains bullish on uranium, as quoted on Financial Review, forecasting a price surge of over 50% to $120 per pound this year, with further increases to $135 in 2026 and $140 in 2027. Morgan Stanley also follows suit, reinforcing its bullish stance on uranium, at least for 2025.”, Yashwardhan Jain wrote for Zacks earlier this year, as Nasdaq.com reported.

The Uranium Production In Niger

The DASA uranium production project is located 95 km north of Agadez, Niger, and the processing plant is scheduled to begin operations in the second quarter of 2026. With U3O8 produced at an AISC of $35.70/lb according to the 2024 feasibility study, DASA will be a financially sound uranium producer, as it will be able to resell the U3O8 at a much higher price per pound (at the time of writing, the price was $71.60/lb, and the previously illustrated LT price forecasts take it even higher).

Commenting on Global Atomic's first-quarter 2025 results, President and CEO Stephen G. Roman said about the development of DASA:

"During the first quarter of 2025, progress continued at the Dasa Project. Earthworks and civil works are advancing and large components for the process plant are now arriving on site. Transport through Nigeria has proven to be an effective alternative route, which gives us more flexibility in bringing materials and equipment to site."

"In early May, we achieved a significant milestone of 1,000 days with no lost time injury, which is a testament to the strong safety culture that governs our operations in Niger."

DASA is expected to produce an average of 2.9 million pounds of U3O8 per year from an underground uranium deposit over a 23-year mine life. As the CEO suggested, acceptable transportation routes to markets and suppliers exert mitigating effects on the investment risk.

High-grade Zinc Oxide Concentrates Division

The 49 percent stake in the joint venture Befesa Silvermet Turkey, S.L. ("BST"), which operates a modern zinc recycling plant in Iskenderun, Turkey, is developing well from a productivity perspective: On a 100 percent basis, 18,608 tonnes of EAFD (“Electric Arc Furnace Dust”) were processed, slightly less than the 19,990 EAFD in the same quarter of the previous year, but the company delivered a 3 percent year-on-year higher production of 6,440 DMT (“Dry Tons”) in Q1-2025.

In the first quarter of 2025, the Turkish joint venture sold less zinc than it produced, namely approximately 6.5 million pounds of zinc concentrate. This represents a 30% decrease compared to the previous year. Nevertheless, profitability improved due to the very positive development of the average zinc price on the LME markets, which increased by 16.2% year-on-year to US$ 1.29 per pound in the first quarter of 2025.

The company reports that (all amounts in CA$):

“The Company's share of EBITDA was $1.5 million in Q1 2025 ($0.7 million in Q1 2024) and the Company's equity share of net income was $0.3 million ($0.3 million in Q1 2024).”

This profitability was supported by Global Atomic's share of revenue of CA$ 0.29 million in Q1 2025, an increase of 7.4% year-over-year.

Commenting on Global Atomic's first-quarter 2025 results, President and CEO Stephen G. Roman said about the 49 per cent stake in the BST Joint Venture in Turkey:

“Our Turkish zinc joint venture has returned to profitability, even though steel mills in the region who supply our primary raw material, electric arc furnace dust, are operating at only 70% of their designed capacity.”

Looking ahead, the company expects a gradual improvement in the performance of the Turkish joint venture in 2025, as steel mill activity in the region returns to pre-COVID levels. This, combined with lower input costs, should help ensure profitability is at least as good as in the first quarter of 2025. The company expects zinc prices to be subject to significant fluctuations in the wake of the global trade war triggered by President Trump's tariffs on US imports. Forecasts for the future zinc price are therefore subject to considerable uncertainty.

Zinc futures (LMZSDS03:COM) were trading at US$2,778.50/tonne, or approximately US$1.26 per pound, at the time of writing, representing a decline of 7.70% year-to-date and 9% over the past 12 months.

The most recent forecast for 2025 anticipated a decline in zinc prices. Analysts expect a price downtrend to approximately US$2,668 per tonne, or approximately US$1.21 per pound, with the estimate challenged by Trump's tariffs on US imports and, in particular, his bitter trade war with China. But subsequently, the dramatic pause in the trade war between the US and China marked a first step towards a gradual resumption of trade talks between the 2 economic superpowers. Against this backdrop, analysts are likely to lift up their zinc price predictions. Zinc futures are currently trading at US$2,778.50/tonne or approximately US$1.26/pound, indicating a new, more optimistic view of zinc prices and a return to the average price achieved by Global Atomic in the first quarter of 2025.

We believe that profitability, which is an important factor in US equity sentiment, at Global Atomic is on track to ensure that we do not regret the positive results the company has already delivered in the first quarter of 2025.

The Liquidity Of The Company: On Track To Lay More Solid Groundwork For Uranium And Zinc Mineral Projects

Global Atomic had cash on hand of CAD 21.4 million as of March 31, 2025, including gross proceeds of CA$ 35.6 million from the closing of the private placement at the end of January 2025, under which 44,500,000 units of the company (= one common share of the company and one common share purchase warrant) were offered to the market at a price of CA$ 0.80 per unit.

The key measures of Global Atomic's balance sheet are as follows:

Global Atomic's 49% interest in the zinc production joint venture generated revenue of CA$ 286,757, but net income was negative CA$ 3,992,760, resulting in a potential cash burn of CA$ 4.2 million for the Q1-2025. With cash on hand of approximately CA$21.4 million, the balance sheet shows that operations can be financed for just over 12 months (administrative expenses plus financing costs and potential foreign exchange losses). The company can extend this period, if necessary, by offering common shares, thereby increasing its liquidity. Based on the past, this seems the company's standard practice. There appear to be no significant issues in securing sufficient and available funds for Global Atomic’s various expenditure obligations. Also, we would like to point out to investors that the first quarter of 2025 was marked by unusual turbulence. We believe this is due to the significant foreign exchange loss of C$2.93 million that Global Atomic recorded at the end of the first quarter of 2025. In the previous quarter, when the market was less volatile and uncertain, the company achieved a gain of C$3.75 million. And President Trump's threat of tariffs is likely the factor of unusual volatility and uncertainty compared to all the times when the situation was much calmer, more “business as usual,” without Trump potentially triggering a trade war, and the ground was less fertile for those unpredictable outcomes that often translate into damages regarding the foreign exchange markets.

Now, while nothing is certain in financial markets, especially in these highly volatile geopolitical and economic times, the situation appears to be easing as the tariff war gives way to talks between the United States and other global economies, particularly China, which has so far been an exciting chapter in the global trade war. The foreign exchange market could already be reflecting a return to a more relaxed climate between the trading partners involved globally. As the development of the relationship between the US dollar and the Canadian dollar shows, the peak in mid-Q1 2025 is well behind the current downward trend, promoting an economically positive outcome among possible events in the foreign exchange market to the benefit of Global Atomic Corp.'s balance sheet.

In our view, concerns should focus less on the means to continue financing the balance sheet and more on the impact on market sentiment of cosmetic effects on earnings per share resulting from a higher number of shares outstanding due to new equity issuance, as earnings are a key driver of US equity markets.

However, such negative effects on Global Atomic's share price could be effectively mitigated if:

  • The investment in the uranium mining project in Niger is further developed into a U308 production facility for nuclear power plants. Then this asset begins to generate income, which would represent a relevant part of Global Atomic's overall return, as the company holds an 80% stake in the investment.
  • The market discussion refocuses on the promising future of zinc and other base metals as a key element of the energy transition. This topic has cooled off somewhat recently due to concerns about the impact of the trade war on global growth. Based on A) and B), the profit potential for Global Atomic would outweigh the negative consequences of share dilution.

Also, the company's financial position is solid, as evidenced by its Altman Z Score of 9.37 (see the “Risk” section of this Seeking Alpha website). This is above the safety level of 3.0 and does not pose any risk of default within a few years.

The company is well-positioned to build an even more solid financial base, provided it continues to deliver strong profitability results, which, as mentioned in the previous sections of this article, appears likely, and as the company does not rule out the efforts to develop alternative financing channels.

About its 80 percent stake in the Dasa project in Niger:

  1. A credit line with the U.S. Development Bank to finance 60% of Dasa's development costs should be available starting in the second quarter of 2025.
  2. A third party could become a minority shareholder in the DASA uranium mining project in Niger bringing additional liquidity to Global Atomic's balance sheet. The DASA project is valued based on its intrinsic value, not the market price of Global Atomic's OTCQX-listed shares with a bias toward appreciation given the strong prospects for uranium as part of clean energy diversification strategies in established economies. And with the DASA uranium project’s intrinsic value also on track to increase as funds from the U.S. Development Bank are spent on the project. Due to this possible scenario, following the announcement of the U.S. Development Bank's creation of the credit facility, there could be a positive impact on Global Atomic' share price.

About its 49% interest in the joint venture in the zinc business in Turkey:

Global Atomic does not operate the zinc recycling plant in the BST joint venture in Iskenderun, Turkey, but its 51% joint venture partner, Befesa Zinc S.A.U. (Befesa), does. This eliminates significant costs for Global Atomic, which Befesa will bear instead. To clarify whether Befesa can financially continue the BST joint venture, we would refer to Befesa's analysis. In the context of this article, Global Atomic's statement in its Q1 2025 report leaves no doubt that Befesa is a reliable and financially trustworthy partner, which we believe offers more than sufficient condition for the stability of Global Atomic's balance sheet.

Global Atomic Corp. states:

“Befesa is a market leader in EAFD recycling, with approximately 50% of the European EAFD market and facilities located throughout Europe, Asia and the United States of America.”

Befesa S.A. (OTCPK:BFSAF) is a company with annual revenue of US$1.35 billion, a market capitalization of US$1.24 billion, and a dividend payout of US$31.6 million in the past year.

Befesa finances its activities through debt as well. After deducting cash and cash equivalents and short-term investments of US$ 113.6 million, net debt amounts to US$ 662.9 million. The interest coverage ratio of 3.3 (= 12-month operating income of US$ 139 million to 12-month interest expense of US$ 42 million), compared to a minimum of 1.5 to 2.0, indicates the sustainability of maturing liabilities. The net debt-to-EBITDA ratio is 3x, compared to a maximum acceptable value of 4 to 5.

Despite volatility due to commodity markets, Befesa S.A.'s free cash flow (per share) has been positive for years and has increased in recent years.

Stock Price: Levels Support A Buy Rating

The consolidation of uranium prices is viewed as creating an environment for buying opportunities in the uranium equity universe.

Zinc prices, as previously discussed in this analysis, are weaker compared to last year.

Since Global Atomic's shares are exposed to both minerals, the resulting headwinds could be making them more attractively valued as of this writing.

Global Atomic Corp., listed on the OTCQX under the ticker symbol "GLATF," traded at $0.66 per share, representing a market capitalization of $210.06 million. Shares are trading almost entirely below the MA Ribbon and well below the $1.05 per share midpoint of the 52-week range of $0.30 to $1.80 per share.

With the trade war potentially impacting uranium prices, pessimism could drag Global Atomic shares lower. With an RSI of 55.43, there is certainly room for a downside, making for a more attractive entry point or share price.

But it is also true that as Global Atomic's long-term position would benefit from a very positive outlook, especially for uranium, so investors could add to the stock from this level already if they wish.

Risk Section

Holding a position in Global Atomic involves risk, which has been addressed so far in this analysis, particularly in the section on the company's liquidity and the potential running out of cash resources. Here in this section, we will add a few risk factors:

  • On the US stock exchange OTCQX, Global Atomic Corp. shares trade under the symbol "GLATF" as a not very liquid stock, as evidenced by the average volume (3 months) of only 425,962 out of 308.09 million shares outstanding (ticker) (see the “Trading Data” section on this page of the Seeking Alpha website). If an investor with an oversized position needs to reduce it quickly and significantly due to new circumstances, the low daily share volume can hinder the efficient execution of the mission with the risk of ultimately suffering losses as well.
  • Many African countries typically struggle with adversities such as political instability, social unrest, geophysical phenomena, and climatic conditions that sometimes place a severe strain on people's physical strength. Despite these challenging conditions, investors will be pleased to hear that these problems are being effectively mitigated in Niger, where the company is investing in the uranium mining project. The Niger government seems very positive and supportive of the DASA project, and Global Atomic also observes high employee motivation.

The company reports that numerous infrastructure problems are being solved in Niger and explains:

“Logistical constraints, such as border closures, are being actively addressed at the highest levels of government, with alternative shipping routes being secured via Algeria, Nigeria, and Togo/Burkina Faso.

This continued government support ensures that the project will not face regulatory hurdles or material operational delays, limiting the risks due to external geopolitical factors.”

  • With respect to the 49% interest in the joint venture Befesa Silvermet Turkey, S.L. (“BST”), based on our experience with other companies in Turkey, we note that the country generally has a favorable environment for conducting mining and exploration activities, thus mitigating geophysical factors such as seismic events occurring every now and then.
  • The 90-day tariff truce began in early April 2025 with the goal of restoring cooperative trade relations between the United States and major economies within that timeframe. If this goal fails, concerns about global trade could resurge, and the negative impact on zinc and uranium could weigh on Global Atomic's balance sheet. The dynamic is based on past experience, which goes something like this: When analysts downgrade profit potential in the face of more moderate zinc and uranium prices, the market takes note and, as usual, may vent its gloomiest sentiments on the U.S. stock market, not sparing Global Atomic's shares. Against this backdrop, as previously mentioned, tensions between global trading partners appear to be headed for a happy ending.
  • In the collective imaginary, uranium is associated with nuclear power, but also with nuclear weapons. Particularly with regard to the latter category, the focus is on a geopolitical climate that is constantly on the verge of becoming more tense – in light of the Ukraine conflict, the crisis in the Middle East or the tensions between the US and Iran over the latter’s nuclear ambitions. Should the global situation become less motivating for bankers, a reduction in the planned capacity expansion of nuclear power plants would lead to lower demand for uranium to power the plants than previously expected. In an environment of declining interest, uranium prices generally suffer somewhat, and if prices decline, this trend could impact Global Atomic's market value in two ways: A) directly through its shares, as they are positively correlated with the price of U308 raw material. B) indirectly, by raising lower-than-expected capital through the potential sale of a minority stake in the Dasa uranium mining project, as the third party would pay less due to the lower intrinsic value of the Dasa project if uranium prices fluctuate negatively.

Conclusion

Global Atomic Corp. is a Canadian company that is developing uranium resources in Niger for nuclear power plants. Driven by an increase in electricity consumption from the proliferation of energy-intensive data centers empowered with artificial intelligence, experts expect higher demand for uranium to power more nuclear power plants. In our opinion, it is not the AI-data centers that should fuel the optimism about uranium demand, but rather the construction of new nuclear reactors or the extension of their lifetimes in order to diversify energy sources. The latter is done by prioritizing cleaner energy sources and reducing dependence on certain countries that, despite being rich in fossil fuels, are seen as contributing to geopolitical tensions, threats and instability.

Global Atomic Corp. also owns a 49% stake in a zinc production joint venture in Turkey. As zinc is a key metal for electrification, the use of electric vehicles and AI-powered data centers and infrastructure upgrade projects, the promising outlook for zinc is yet another reason to be bullish on Global Atomic's stock with a long-term vision.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
